issues_found: Baseline modality mismatch undermines the stated hypothesis: The hypothesis claims the multimodal model (text + image + sequences) outperforms a 'text-only' baseline, but the XGBoost baseline actually includes user sequence features (prior click count, cart count, recency, category diversity). This means neither model is truly 'text-only', and the experiment is not a clean ablation of multimodal vs. text-only. The hypothesis and the baseline definition are inconsistent with each other.

Architecture asymmetry confounds attribution: The proposed model is a deep transformer with cross-attention, learned embeddings, and end-to-end training, while the baseline is a GBT on hand-crafted aggregates. Any observed performance difference could be attributed to model capacity, inductive biases, or representational power rather than the multimodal signal specifically. A fairer comparison would include a transformer baseline trained on the same non-image features, isolating the image and joint-encoding contribution.

The hypothesis tests a bundle, not image signal: The experiment conflates at least three changes simultaneously — (1) adding image embeddings, (2) using a transformer architecture instead of GBTs, and (3) using learned sequence representations via attention rather than aggregated statistics. Without ablations (text+sequence transformer, text-only transformer, multimodal transformer), it is impossible to attribute any lift to the multimodal component specifically, which is the stated hypothesis.

Image features are described inconsistently as both multimodal signal and as excluded from the baseline 'to isolate the hypothesis', yet the baseline also lacks the architectural capacity to use sequential signals properly. These are two separate differences being smuggled into a single comparison, making the experiment uninterpretable if results are positive or negative.

BPE tokenizer fit on training vocabulary only creates an uneven playing field for cold-start products: Cold-start products (≤90 days old at evaluation time) may use vocabulary, brand names, or style descriptors that emerged after month 12. A frozen training-set vocabulary will systematically degrade text representations for genuinely new products, which are precisely the items in the evaluation set. This introduces a bias that could understate the text signal and artificially favor or disfavor the transformer depending on how gracefully it handles unknown tokens.

Micro-averaging NDCG@10 across sessions may be misleading: Micro-averaging allows sessions with many cold-start impressions to dominate the metric. Sessions with more impressions tend to correspond to more engaged users or popular categories, potentially introducing a selection bias into the primary metric. Macro-averaging across sessions (or users) would provide a more conservative and representative estimate of population-level performance.

Negative sampling strategy is underspecified: The design states negatives are 'sampled stratified by product age-group and category', but does not specify the positive-to-negative ratio, whether sampling is done at the session level or globally, or how this interacts with the label definition (all non-clicked impressions are negatives). Different sampling ratios can substantially affect calibration and ranking metrics, and without fixing this identically for both models, the comparison may be unfair.

Cold-start definition creates a temporal inconsistency: Products are defined as cold-start if they are ≤90 days old at evaluation time, but training data spans months 1–12. A product listed in month 10 would appear in training as a warm product (with accumulated clicks) and then re-appear in the test set as a 'cold-start' product if it was somehow relisted or if the age window overlaps. More importantly, products listed in months 13–18 will appear only in validation or test, meaning the model has never seen interaction data for these products during training, which is appropriate, but the design does not explicitly confirm that training excludes future-listed products' interaction signals from month 1–12 impression logs.

Validation set leakage risk via hyperparameter search scope: The XGBoost grid search spans 27 hyperparameter combinations evaluated on the validation set. For the transformer, early stopping criteria and presumably architecture choices (heads, layers, dimensions) are also tuned on validation. If the transformer hyperparameter search space is larger or iterated more aggressively than XGBoost's grid, the transformer benefits from more implicit validation-set fitting, creating an asymmetric overfitting risk to the validation set.

AUC-ROC on the 'full test set' as a diagnostic metric is problematic: The primary evaluation is restricted to cold-start products, but AUC-ROC is computed on the full test set including warm products. This is described as a diagnostic for distribution shift, but interpreting it alongside cold-start-specific metrics introduces confusion. A warm-start AUC uplift could mask cold-start degradation or vice versa, and mixing these populations without clear separation is methodologically sloppy for a stated cold-start experiment.

Absolute positional encoding for user sequences is a questionable design choice that is not acknowledged: User click and cart histories are truncated to the last 20 events and given absolute positional encodings, but the sequences are already time-ordered with relative timestamp offsets as features. Absolute positional encoding imposes an assumption that position 1 always means 'oldest retained event', which is not invariant to sequence length. Relative positional encodings or rotary embeddings would be more principled for variable-length behavioral sequences, and this choice should at minimum be acknowledged as a limitation.

No statistical significance testing is specified: The design reports NDCG@10 as a point estimate with no mention of confidence intervals, bootstrap resampling, or significance tests. Given that cold-start products are a subset of the test set and fashion data has high variance, a point estimate difference in NDCG@10 could be within noise. Without a plan for significance testing, the experiment cannot reliably distinguish a real effect from sampling variance.

Single frozen image encoder limits generalizability claims: ResNet50 is acknowledged as a design choice made to isolate the hypothesis, but using a single, potentially outdated image encoder (ResNet50 vs. modern vision transformers like ViT or CLIP) means that a null result on image signal could reflect encoder quality rather than the value of image modality. The scope limitations section mentions this, but it weakens the practical value of the experiment significantly — a negative result is uninterpretable with respect to image features.
smoke_response.reasoning: The experiment has sound temporal split discipline and reasonable metric selection, but it fundamentally fails to test its stated hypothesis because the comparison conflates modality differences with architectural and representational differences simultaneously, making any result unattributable to multimodal signal specifically. Several additional concerns around micro-averaging, negative sampling underspecification, vocabulary leakage for cold-start items, and the absence of statistical significance testing further undermine the reliability and interpretability of conclusions that would be drawn from this design.
